memes.label: A Man Is Known by the Company He Keeps
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that character is revealed by one's chosen associates — an Ass bought at market immediately selects the laziest companion in the stable, and the purchaser returns him on the spot without further testing.

memes.label: Advice
memes.description: Counsel offered by one party to another — in Aesop's Fables a double-edged tool that can save or destroy, distinguished not by its content but by the character and intentions of its giver and the wisdom of its receiver.

memes.label: Anger
memes.description: "A violent emotional reaction to perceived injury — in Aesop's Fables, anger is always a trap: it consumes the angry, blinds them to danger, and delivers them into the hands of the very creatures they despise."

memes.label: The Ass
memes.description: The beast of burden in Aesop's Fables — hardworking but foolish, the Ass embodies the consequences of envying others' roles, mistaking appearance for substance, and forgetting one's own nature.

memes.label: Attempt Not Beyond Your Power
memes.description: "A Jackdaw tries to imitate an Eagle by seizing a ram, gets its claws tangled in the wool, and is captured by the Shepherd — earning not only failure but ridicule for pretending to be what it is not."
aliases: if you attempt what is beyond your power

memes.label: Bad Companions Corrupt Good Character
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that associating with wrongdoers will cause others to judge you as one of them — a Stork caught among crop-destroying Cranes is condemned alongside them regardless of his own innocence, for identity is assigned by the company one keeps.

memes.label: The Bat
memes.description: A night-flying mammal appearing in four of Aesop's Fables as the archetype of the double-dealer — switching allegiance between birds and beasts as convenience dictates, and ultimately shunned by both.

memes.label: The Battle Is Not Always to the Strong
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that physical strength does not guarantee victory — a Mouse repeatedly bites a Bull who exhausts himself charging the wall, and the small creature's taunt from safety is the moral's verdict: the advantage goes to the one who cannot be reached.

memes.label: Be Content with Your Lot
memes.description: "A Crab abandons the seashore for an inland meadow and is caught by a hungry Fox. As he is about to be eaten, he admits: 'This is just what I deserve — I had no business leaving my natural home.' The moral warns that discontent with one's proper place invites disaster."
aliases: do not envy what you cannot have

memes.label: Be Shy of Favours Bestowed at the Expense of Others
memes.description: "A Cook throws a strange Dog out of the kitchen window during a feast. The moral warns against accepting hospitality or favours that come at another person's expense — such generosity is not genuine and the recipient may find themselves paying the price meant for someone else."
aliases: shy of favours at others expense
beware of gifts at another's cost
do not accept favours that harm third parties

memes.label: The Bee
memes.description: The social insect of Aesop's Fables — industrious and self-sacrificing, whose gift of stings from Jupiter becomes a double-edged blessing that kills the bee as surely as it wounds its enemy.

memes.label: Betray a Friend and You Ruin Yourself
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that treachery against an ally rebounds on the traitor — a Fox who betrays his partner the Ass to a Lion is himself devoured first, because the Lion judges that a creature who would betray his own companion is not worth keeping promises to.

memes.label: Better Poverty Without Care
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that freedom from obligation outweighs material wealth — a Fir-tree envies the lowly Bramble when the axe comes, and the lesson reverberates through fables of the discontented and the content.
aliases: better poverty without a care than wealth with obligations

memes.label: Better Servitude With Safety
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that security under a master is preferable to freedom with mortal risk — a Fox who resents the Lion's unfair share of the hunt learns that self-sufficiency carries dangers servitude never did.
aliases: better servitude with safety than freedom with danger

memes.label: Beware Those Who Can Harm from a Distance
memes.description: The Aesopic warning to give a wide berth to those who can inflict harm from beyond your reach — the Lion flees the Archer not because of the arrow's sting but because of what the messenger implies about the one who sent it.

memes.label: Blame
memes.description: The assignment of fault — Aesop's Fables treat blame as a directional weapon whose true target reveals the blamer's character more than the blamed's guilt.

memes.label: Boasters Brag Most When They Cannot Be Detected
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that those who boast most loudly do so only when their claims cannot be verified — the Monkey invents illustrious ancestors in a graveyard where no corpse can rise to contradict him.

memes.label: Boasting
memes.description: The act of proclaiming one's own excellence — Aesop's Fables treat boasting as a reliable signal of insecurity and foreshadow of humiliation, with every boaster eventually exposed.

memes.label: Bred in the Bone Comes Out in the Flesh
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that innate nature overrides nurture: a Wolf's Cub raised among dogs reverts to wolfish behavior as an adult, and no amount of training can overwrite what is bred in the bone.

memes.label: Cleverness
memes.description: The capacity for inventive problem-solving and strategic thinking — Aesop's Fables celebrate cleverness when it serves survival but warn of its self-destructive excess.

memes.label: The Cock
memes.description: The herald of dawn in Aesop's Fables — a vigilant bird whose crowing rouses honest men to work but makes him the target of thieves, predators, and the ungrateful servants who kill him for waking them too early.

memes.label: They Complain Most Who Suffer Least
memes.description: The Aesopic observation that those who contribute the least often make the most noise about their burdens — the groaning Axletrees drown out the silently straining Oxen who bear the real weight.

memes.label: Contempt
memes.description: The conviction of one's own superiority so complete that others are beneath notice — Aesop's Fables treat contempt as the most dangerous of emotions because it is invisible to the one who feels it, and it guarantees that the contemptuous will be blindsided by those they dismiss.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: The willingness to face danger despite fear — Aesop's Fables distinguish true courage, grounded in skill and prudence, from reckless bravado that crumbles at the first test.

memes.label: The Cow
memes.description: The livestock animal of Aesop's Fables — a creature of agricultural life, passive and productive, appearing at the margins of human follies rather than as a protagonist, representing the uncomplaining domestic animal whose labor sustains others.

memes.label: Coward
memes.description: The yielding to fear when action is required — Aesop's Fables expose the coward as one who boasts of courage in safety but flees at the first sign of danger, and whose words are rendered worthless by his own legs.

memes.label: Credit Thieves Get Punished
memes.description: 'An Ass carries a sacred Image through town. People bow in reverence to the Image, but the Ass assumes the homage is for him, grows conceited, and refuses to move — whereupon his driver beats him, saying: "Do you suppose men pay worship to an Ass?" Those who claim credit not their own invite humiliation.'

memes.label: The Crow
memes.description: A clever but vain corvid appearing in six of Aesop's Fables — most famously in "The Crow and the Pitcher" where her ingenuity saves her from thirst, and in "The Fox and the Crow" where her vanity costs her a meal.

memes.label: Cunning
memes.description: The use of intelligence for deceptive or predatory ends — in Aesop's Fables, cunning is the weapon of the weak against the strong, but also a force that, unchecked, destroys entire communities.

memes.label: Danger
memes.description: The state of exposure to harm, injury, or loss — a pervasive condition in Aesop's Fables that reveals character, distinguishes true friends from false ones, and tests the prudence of every creature.

memes.label: A Known Danger Cannot Deceive Again
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that past experience of danger inoculates against future deception — a Cat who hangs herself from a peg pretending to be dead fools no Mouse, for the Mice have learned that whatever innocent guise danger wears, the substance beneath is unchanged.

memes.label: Dearly Bought Advantages Are Doubtful Blessings
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that advantages acquired at high cost — freedom traded for security, leisure swapped for dignity — are seldom worth their price: the Wild Ass envies the Pack-Ass's sleek coat only until he sees the beating that pays for it.
aliases: what costs much may not be worth it

memes.label: Deception
memes.description: The act of misleading others through false appearances or words — Aesop's Fables distinguish between self-deception, which is always fatal, and deception of others, which carries variable moral weight depending on motive.

memes.label: Deeds, Not Words
memes.description: The Aesopic demand for action over assertion — a Traveler boasts of a prodigious jump he performed in Rhodes, but a bystander challenges him to perform it here and now, exposing the gap between claim and capability.

memes.label: Discontent
memes.description: "A restless dissatisfaction with one's present condition — in Aesop's Fables, discontent is the engine of self-destruction: those who cannot accept their lot invariably trade it for a worse one."

memes.label: Disguise
memes.description: The act of altering one's appearance to deceive — in Aesop's Fables, disguise is a fragile strategy that consistently backfires, exposing the wearer to worse consequences than honesty would have incurred.

memes.label: The Dishonest Get No Credit
memes.description: "A Wolf accuses a Fox of theft before an Ape. The Ape judges that the Wolf never lost what he claims, yet also believes the Fox is guilty — because the dishonest get no credit, even when they tell the truth."
aliases: dishonest get no credit even if they act honestly

memes.label: Dishonesty
memes.description: In Aesop's Fables, dishonesty is a practical liability — false faith may escape human judgment but cannot escape its own consequences, and those who lie for advantage are ultimately undone by their own deceptions.

memes.label: Do Not Attempt Too Much at Once
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that greed causes loss — a Boy trying to grasp too many filberts from a jar cannot withdraw his hand, and only by releasing half does he regain his freedom.
aliases: don't bite off more than you can chew
half a loaf is better than none

memes.label: Do Not Count Your Chickens
memes.description: The Aesopic warning against premature celebration and counting on outcomes not yet realized.
aliases: do not count your chickens before they are hatched

memes.label: Do Not Promise More Than You Can Perform
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that overpromising invites divine or natural retribution — a sick man vows a hundred oxen to the gods, recovers, and substitutes tallow miniatures, only to be sold into slavery for exactly the hundred crowns he sought.
aliases: don't make promises you can't keep

memes.label: Do Not Waste Your Pity on a Scamp
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that mercy is wasted on those who are inherently harmful — a Flea pleads for its life on the grounds of being too small to cause harm, but the Man refuses to spare any creature that does deliberate wrong.

memes.label: The Dog
memes.description: The domestic companion of Aesop's Fables — loyal but sometimes foolish, appearing as guard, pet, predator, and symbol of the tension between wild nature and servitude.

memes.label: Envy
memes.description: A destructive desire for what others possess — in Aesop's Fables, envy consistently leads the envious to self-destruction while leaving the envied unharmed.

memes.label: Equals Make the Best Friends
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that friendship across unequal terms is inherently fragile — an Earthenware Pot refuses a Brazen Pot's offer of protection because proximity alone will destroy the weaker vessel.

memes.label: Every Man for Himself
memes.description: When the citizens of a city debate the best material for their fortifications, a Carpenter recommends wood, a Stone-mason urges stone, and a Tanner insists on leather — each arguing for the material that serves his own trade. The moral that "Every man for himself" is not a celebration of self-reliance but a diagnosis of how private interest masquerades as public counsel.

memes.label: Evil Tendencies Are Shown Early
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that character reveals itself in youth — a Blind Man, handed a Wolf's Cub, cannot identify the species by touch but knows immediately that the creature cannot be trusted in a sheepfold.
aliases: the leopard cannot change his spots

memes.label: Evil Wishes Come Home to Roost
memes.description: The Aesopic warning that malevolent wishes rebound upon the wisher — a Bee asks Jupiter for stings that kill honey-thieves, and Jupiter grants them, but makes the stings fatal to the bee herself.
aliases: what you wish on others returns to you

memes.label: Example Is Better Than Precept
memes.description: The Aesopic principle that demonstrating how to do something is more effective than telling — an Old Crab who cannot walk straight herself has no authority to correct her son's sideways gait.
